title: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia
authors: William H. Sauer, Usha B. Tedrow

items.subtopic: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin
items.title: Characteristics of Right Ventricular Outflow Tract VT
items.content: Idiopathic VT originating from the right ventricular outflow tract presents with a left bundle branch block pattern in V1, an inferior axis, tall QRS complexes in the inferior leads, and late transition in the precordial leads.
keyPoints: Left bundle branch block pattern in V1
Inferior axis
Tall QRS in inferior leads
Late transition in precordial leads
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin

items.subtopic: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin
items.title: Characteristics of Left Ventricular Outflow Tract VT
items.content: Idiopathic VT originating from the left ventricular outflow tract shows an inferiorly directed axis with early precordial transition and a prominent R wave in V2-V3.
keyPoints: Inferiorly directed axis
Early precordial transition
Prominent R wave in V2-V3
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin

items.subtopic: C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. Repaired tetralogy of Fallot | 6. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | 7. Genetic arrhythmia syndromes | 8. Idiopathic polymorphic VT or ventricular fibrillation
items.title: Identifying Monomorphic and Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia Origins
items.content: Differentiating the origins of monomorphic and polymorphic ventricular tachycardias (VT) is crucial for appropriate management. Specific ECG patterns can indicate the origin, such as left bundle branch morphology in V1 for arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy and repaired tetralogy of Fallot.
keyPoints: Monomorphic VT usually originates from the right ventricle with left bundle branch morphology in V1 (Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy).
Repaired tetralogy of Fallot often shows monomorphic VT of RV origin, typically with left bundle branch morphology in Vt.
Idiopathic polymorphic VT or VF is commonly triggered by premature ventricular contractions from the left posterior fascicle.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. items.subtopic: C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. Repaired tetralogy of Fallot | 6. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | 7. Genetic arrhythmia syndromes | 8. Idiopathic polymorphic VT or ventricular fibrillation
items.title: Role of ECG in Distinguishing Ventricular Tachycardia Types
items.content: ECG criteria are essential for distinguishing between supraventricular tachycardia with aberrancy and ventricular tachycardia. VA dissociation is a reliable marker for VT, while specific QRS morphology patterns can suggest VT over other causes.
keyPoints: VA dissociation indicates VT when the atrial rate is slower than the ventricular rate.
Monophasic R wave or Rs complex in aVR and concordance from V1 to V6 are relatively specific for VT.
QRS morphology similar during tachycardia as in sinus rhythm suggests supraventricular tachycardia, but is not absolute.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. items.subtopic: C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. Repaired tetralogy of Fallot | 6. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | 7. Genetic arrhythmia syndromes | 8. Idiopathic polymorphic VT or ventricular fibrillation
items.title: Evaluating Structural and Genetic Causes of Ventricular Tachycardia
items.content: Structural heart diseases, such as ischemic cardiomyopathy, and genetic conditions can predispose individuals to specific types of VT. Understanding these associations aids in diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: Ischemic cardiomyopathy often presents with polymorphic VT and VF, necessitating an evaluation for ischemia.
Nonischemic cardiomyopathies like sarcoidosis or familial arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathies can cause monomorphic VT due to fibrotic scars.
Genetic syndromes such as Long QT syndrome (Torsades de pointes) and Brugada syndrome are associated with specific types of polymorphic VT.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. Repaired tetralogy of Fallot | 6. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | 7. Genetic arrhythmia syndromes | 8. Idiopathic polymorphic VT or ventricular fibrillation

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS
items.title: When to Perform QRS Synchronous Electrical Cardioversion in Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia
items.content: QRS synchronous electrical cardioversion is indicated for sustained ventricular tachycardia if the patient presents with hypotension, impaired consciousness, or pulmonary edema. It should be performed after sedation if the patient is conscious.
keyPoints: Indications: Hypotension, impaired consciousness, pulmonary edema
QRS synchronous electrical cardioversion recommended
Sedation advised before cardioversion if patient is conscious
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS
items.title: Use of Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillators (ICDs) in Sustained Monomorphic VT
items.content: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators are warranted for secondary prevention of sudden death in patients with sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia associated with structural heart disease.
keyPoints: Indication: Secondary prevention of sudden death
Patient population: Sustained monomorphic VT with structural heart disease
ICDs recommended
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.title: ICD Indication and Efficacy in Coronary Artery Disease with Sustained VT
items.content: Patients with sustained monomorphic VT due to coronary artery disease often have a history of large MI. Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs) are indicated for secondary prevention if there is reasonable survival expectation, reducing annual mortality from 12.3% to 8.8% and arrhythmic deaths by 50%. Antiarrhythmic drugs alone do not improve survival.
keyPoints: 70% of patients with VT have recurrence within 2 years
ICDs reduce annual mortality from 12.3% to 8.8%
ICD reduces arrhythmic deaths by 50%
ICD is indicated for secondary prevention if survival expectation is reasonable
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.title: Management of Recurrent VT in Patients with ICDs
items.content: Despite ICD implantation, patients remain at risk for heart failure and recurrent VT. Catheter ablation is preferred over escalated medical therapy (e.g., amiodarone) for managing recurrent VT episodes due to better outcomes.
keyPoints: 5-year mortality exceeds 30% in patients with depressed ejection fraction
Catheter ablation fared better than antiarrhythmic drugs in the VANISH study
ICD therapies (shocks, pacing) are associated with increased heart failure and psychological stress
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.title: Role of Catheter Ablation in VT Management
items.content: Catheter ablation is recommended for patients with ischemic cardiomyopathy failing or intolerant to antiarrhythmic drugs. It is also considered when long-term therapy with drugs like amiodarone, which has significant toxicities, is not desired.
keyPoints: BERLIN VT trial showed fewer VT episodes but more complications in the ablation group
Consensus recommends catheter ablation for ischemic cardiomyopathy failing antiarrhythmic drugs
Consideration of ablation when long-term drug therapy is undesirable
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE

items.subtopic: NONISCHEMIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY | ARRHYTHMOGENIC RIGHT VENTRICULAR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Guidance for VT Ablation in NICM using Imaging Techniques
items.content: In nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy (NICM), catheter ablation of ventricular tachycardia (VT) is less successful compared to ischemic cardiomyopathy. Challenges include the inability to reproduce clinical VT during ablation and complex reentry circuits. Imaging techniques such as MRI or CT with late contrast administration are useful for identifying fibrosis, which can guide effective ablation strategies.
keyPoints: Catheter ablation success rates in NICM are lower than in ischemic cardiomyopathy.
Reproduction of clinical VT and identification of reentry circuits are challenges during endocardial VT ablation in NICM.
MRI or CT with late contrast administration can identify fibrosis to guide ablation.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · NONISCHEMIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY | ARRHYTHMOGENIC RIGHT VENTRICULAR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: NONISCHEMIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY | ARRHYTHMOGENIC RIGHT VENTRICULAR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Characteristics and Management of ARVC-related VT
items.content: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) is a genetic disorder often due to mutations in desmosomal proteins. It presents with palpitations, syncope, or cardiac arrest from sustained monomorphic VT. ECG findings include T-wave inversions in leads V1-V3 and an epsilon wave. Cardiac imaging may reveal right ventricular enlargement or scar tissue. Management includes ICD implantation for VT control, beta-blockers for exercise-induced VT, and catheter ablation which is effective in 70% of cases.
keyPoints: ARVC often involves mutations in desmosomal proteins with autosomal dominant inheritance.
ECG shows T-wave inversions in V1-V3 and epsilon waves; QRS widening (>110 ms) may be present.
ICD is recommended for VT control; catheter ablation prevents/reduces VT episodes in 70% of patients.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · NONISCHEMIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY | ARRHYTHMOGENIC RIGHT VENTRICULAR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
items.title: Risk Factors and Management of Sustained Monomorphic VT in TOF Repairs
items.content: Sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia (VT) is rare but significant in adult congenital heart disease, especially post-repair of tetralogy of Fallot (TOF). Risk factors include age >5 years at repair, high-grade ventricular ectopy, inducible VT on electrophysiologic study, abnormal right ventricular hemodynamics, and sinus rhythm QRS duration >180 ms. An implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) is recommended for patients with spontaneous VT episodes or multiple risk factors.
keyPoints: Prevalence of VT after TOF repair: 3–14%
Risk of sudden cardiac death in adulthood: up to 1% per year by the fourth or fifth decade
Indications for ICD: Spontaneous VT episode or multiple risk factors
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT

items.subtopic: ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
items.title: Characteristics and Treatment of Bundle Branch Reentry VT
items.content: Bundle branch reentry VT occurs in ~5% of patients with monomorphic VT and structural heart disease, involving a reentry circuit through the Purkinje system. The QRS morphology resembles left bundle branch block due to retrograde left bundle and anterograde right bundle conduction. Catheter ablation targeting the right bundle branch can abolish this type of VT.
keyPoints: Prevalence in monomorphic VT with structural heart disease: ~5%
QRS morphology: Resembles left bundle branch block
Treatment: Catheter ablation of the right bundle branch
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT

items.subtopic: ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Idiopathic Monomorphic VT
items.content: Idiopathic monomorphic VT, occurring in patients without structural heart disease, presents with palpitations or lightheadedness. Diagnosis is suggested by QRS morphology and normal sinus rhythm ECG. Cardiac imaging shows no ventricular scar or dysfunction. An ICD is not recommended due to the rarity of sudden death. Treatment focuses on symptom control using beta blockers or calcium channel blockers.
keyPoints: Presentation: Palpitations, lightheadedness
Diagnostic criteria: Normal sinus rhythm ECG and cardiac imaging
ICD recommendation: Not advised for isolated idiopathic VT
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT

items.subtopic: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin
items.question: Which of the following ECG characteristics is associated with right ventricular outflow tract VT?
options: Left bundle branch block pattern in V1 with inferior axis and late transition in precordial leads
Right bundle branch block pattern in V1 with superior axis and early transition in precordial leads
Normal QRS complex with no specific axis deviation
Biphasic QRS complex in lead II
items.explanation: Right ventricular outflow tract VT typically shows a left bundle branch block pattern in V1, inferior axis, and late transition in the precordial leads.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin

items.subtopic: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin
items.question: Which characteristic is associated with left ventricular outflow tract VT on an ECG?
options: Inferior axis with early precordial transition and prominent R wave in V2-V3
Superior axis with late precordial transition and small R wave in V1-V2
Normal QRS complex with no specific axis deviation
Biphasic QRS complex in lead aVL
items.explanation: Left ventricular outflow tract VT is associated with an inferiorly directed axis and early precordial transition, with a prominent R wave in V2-V3.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin

items.subtopic: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin
items.question: What is a common feature of idiopathic VT in structurally normal hearts?
options: It often deteriorates to ventricular fibrillation.
It occurs due to patchy replacement fibrosis.
It can be due to focal automaticity or reentry involving the Purkinje system.
It is always associated with significant ventricular dysfunction.
items.explanation: Idiopathic VT in structurally normal hearts can occur due to a focal region of automaticity or reentry involving a portion of the Purkinje system.

items.question: Which drug is the treatment of choice if heart disease is present during a case of sustained ventricular tachycardia?
items.explanation: Intravenous amiodarone is the drug of choice if heart disease is present.

items.question: What should be assessed following restoration of sinus rhythm in a patient with sustained ventricular tachycardia?
options: Cardiac biomarkers for evidence of myocardial infarction
items.explanation: Assessment of cardiac biomarkers for evidence of myocardial infarction is appropriate.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS
items.question: When should adenosine not be used in the management of sustained ventricular tachycardia?
options: If the patient has a history of asthma
If the patient is hemodynamically unstable
If the patient has a heart transplant or if the wide-complex rhythm is irregular or unstable
items.explanation: Adenosine should not be used if the patient has a heart transplant or if the wide-complex rhythm is irregular or unstable.

items.question: What is a common history found in patients presenting with sustained monomorphic VT associated with coronary artery disease?
options: History of a remote prior large MI
items.explanation: Patients typically present years after the acute infarct with a remodeled ventricle and markedly depressed LV function.

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.question: What is the approximate percentage of patients with coronary artery disease who experience recurrence of VT within 2 years?
items.explanation: Up to 70% of patients have a recurrence of the arrhythmia within 2 years.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.question: What is the impact of ICD implantation on annual mortality in patients with sustained VT or history of cardiac arrest?
items.explanation: ICDs reduce annual mortality from 12.3% to 8.8% and lower arrhythmic deaths by 50%.

items.question: Which therapy is recommended for patients with ischemic cardiomyopathy failing or intolerant of antiarrhythmic drugs?
items.explanation: The most recent consensus statement strongly recommends catheter ablation for these patients.

items.subtopic: ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
items.question: Which of the following is a common substrate for sustained ventricular tachycardia (VT) in patients with adult congenital heart disease?
options: Repairs of ventricular septal defect, particularly tetralogy of Fallot
items.explanation: The most common substrate for sustained VT in adult congenital heart disease is seen in those with repairs of a ventricular septal defect, particularly tetralogy of Fallot.

items.question: What is the typical mechanism for monomorphic VT in patients with repaired tetralogy of Fallot?
options: Reentry around areas of surgically created scar in the left ventricle
Focal automaticity in the right atrium
Reentry around areas of surgically created scar in the right ventricle
Micro-reentrant circuits in the interventricular septum
items.explanation: Monomorphic VT in tetralogy of Fallot most commonly occurs in stereotyped circuits due to reentry around areas of surgically created scar in the right ventricle.

options: Age >5 years at the time of repair
Inducible VT on an electrophysiologic study
items.explanation: The presence of atrial fibrillation is not listed as a risk factor for ventricular tachycardia in patients with repaired tetralogy of Fallot.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS
items.statement: Acute myocardial infarction is a common cause of sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia.
items.explanation: Acute MI is rarely a cause of sustained monomorphic VT.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS
items.statement: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs) are recommended for secondary prevention in patients with structural heart disease and sustained ventricular tachycardia.
items.explanation: ICDs are warranted for secondary prevention of sudden death in these patients.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.statement: Coronary revascularization is likely to prevent recurrent VT in patients with coronary artery disease.
items.explanation: Scar-related reentry is not usually dependent on recurrent acute myocardial ischemia, so coronary revascularization is unlikely to prevent recurrent VT.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.statement: ICD therapies are associated with reduced rates of heart failure and mortality.
items.explanation: ICD therapies, whether shocks or antitachycardia pacing, are associated with increased rates of heart failure, mortality, and psychological stress.

items.statement: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy is most commonly due to mutations in genes encoding for cardiac desmosomal proteins.
items.explanation: ARVC is most commonly due to mutations in genes encoding for cardiac desmosomal proteins.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · NONISCHEMIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY | ARRHYTHMOGENIC RIGHT VENTRICULAR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
items.statement: Sudden death is common in patients with isolated idiopathic ventricular tachycardia.
items.explanation: Sudden death in isolated idiopathic VT is rare, and an ICD is not recommended.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT

items.subtopic: ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
items.statement: Reentry through the Purkinje system is a mechanism for bundle branch reentry ventricular tachycardia.
items.explanation: Bundle branch reentry VT occurs via reentry through the Purkinje system, typically involving retrograde left bundle and anterograde right bundle.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT

items.subtopic: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin
items.assertion: Idiopathic VT can occur in structurally normal hearts.
items.reason: It is due to focal automaticity or reentry involving the Purkinje system.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why idiopathic VT occurs in structurally normal hearts.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia | DIAGNOSIS | 1. Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (VT) without structural heart disease A. Outflow tract origin

items.subtopic: C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. Repaired tetralogy of Fallot | 6. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | 7. Genetic arrhythmia syndromes | 8. Idiopathic polymorphic VT or ventricular fibrillation
items.assertion: Scars are suggested by pathologic Q waves on the ECG in patients with sustained monomorphic VT.
items.reason: Pathologic Q waves indicate myocardial scarring, which is a common cause of sustained monomorphic VT.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. items.subtopic: C. Papillary muscle VT | 2. Ischemic cardiomyopathy | 3. Nonischemic cardiomyopathy | 4. Arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy | 5. Repaired tetralogy of Fallot | 6. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | 7. Genetic arrhythmia syndromes | 8. Idiopathic polymorphic VT or ventricular fibrillation
items.assertion: A P wave following each QRS excludes the diagnosis of VT.
items.reason: 1:1 conduction from ventricle to atrium can occur in VT, making this observation non-exclusive for VT.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because a P wave following each QRS does not exclude VT due to possible 1:1 conduction.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS
items.assertion: Hospitalization and evaluation to define underlying heart disease are required after restoration of sinus rhythm.
items.reason: This is necessary because sustained monomorphic VT often indicates significant underlying cardiac pathology that needs further assessment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.assertion: ICD implantation is indicated for secondary prevention in patients with sustained VT.
items.reason: ICDs reduce annual mortality from 12.3% to 8.8% and lower arrhythmic deaths by 50%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE

items.subtopic: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
items.assertion: Patients with nonischemic cardiomyopathy often have a postviral etiology.
items.reason: Genetic causes are increasingly found in many patients with nonischemic cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE

items.subtopic: NONISCHEMIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY | ARRHYTHMOGENIC RIGHT VENTRICULAR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.assertion: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy can present with a widened QRS in the right precordial leads.
items.reason: Delayed activation of the right ventricle may cause a widened QRS (>110 ms) in the right precordial leads (V1-V3).
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · NONISCHEMIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY | ARRHYTHMOGENIC RIGHT VENTRICULAR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
items.assertion: Idiopathic ventricular tachycardia often presents with palpitations and lightheadedness.
items.reason: The arrhythmia is provoked by sympathetic stimulation or acute withdrawal of sympathetic tone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why idiopathic VT presents with these symptoms.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT

items.subtopic: ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
items.assertion: LV fascicular ventricular tachycardia is typically associated with severe underlying heart disease.
items.reason: It is due to a small reentry circuit in or near the septal ramifications of the LV Purkinje system.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because LV fascicular VT, despite being reentrant, typically has a benign course and is not associated with severe underlying heart disease.
items.reference: Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia · ADULT CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE | BUNDLE BRANCH REENTRY VT | IDIOPATHIC MONOMORPHIC VT | MANAGEMENT OF IDIOPATHIC VT
